_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to construct sustained historical interpretations and arguments using appropriate historical concepts, terms, knowledge, conventions, and evidence from sources.

_source.w: We are learning to construct sustained historical interpretations and arguments using appropriate historical concepts, terms, knowledge, conventions, and evidence from sources.
y: Constructing sustained arguments is the culmination of all historical skills — it demonstrates our ability to think, write, and argue as historians.
This skill requires integrating everything we have learned — source analysis, concept application, evidence evaluation, and clear communication.
The ability to construct sustained, evidence-based arguments is one of the most valued intellectual skills in academic and professional life.
eg: Writing an extended historical essay that presents a clear thesis, deploys evidence systematically, and engages with alternative interpretations.
Constructing a historical argument that uses concepts — causation, significance, continuity and change, perspectives — as analytical tools.
Using evidence from primary and secondary sources to support each stage of our argument.
Acknowledging alternative interpretations and explaining why our own is more persuasive.
Presenting a sustained oral argument supported by multimedia evidence.
Reflecting critically on the strengths and limitations of our interpretation and identifying areas for further investigation.
